evidence: Both `uploadsvc/service.go` and `system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` define private, duplicated action-registry patterns (`actionBindings`/`c2cActionBindings`) with near-identical parse/lookup/execute wiring.

Each module exposes a different local contract-building flow (`ParseAction`/`SupportedActions` vs `ParseC2CAction`/`SupportedC2CActions`) without a shared module boundary for websocket command semantics.

`uploadsvc/adapters/upload_payload_test.go` depends on concrete action/request structure details via field-level assertions, increasing coupling to a specific producer/consumer contract implementation.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: The code paths are coherent and not broken, but duplicated command-translation registries create a maintenance coupling risk: adding or changing action semantics can drift across modules without compile-time enforcement. A shared command-contract boundary or generator-driven registry would improve module coupling.

evidence: Several modules introduce seam abstractions as mutable package-level globals (for test overrides) instead of explicit dependency injection at construction boundaries.

Runtime and orchestration packages define near-parallel wrapper/alias structs (`dockerRoutineRuntime`, `newDockerRoutineRuntime`, `newWireguardRuntime`, `newUrbitRuntime`, etc.) that add indirection without a shared stable interface contract.
Cross-module behavior frequently depends on wide config-shaped inputs and loose assertions rather than bounded domain interfaces, reducing abstraction clarity at package boundaries.

`upload_payload_test.go` only verifies adapter-level mapping (`CommandFromWsPayload`) and does not validate `uploadsvc.Executor` behavior (`NewExecutor`, `Execute`, `DescribeAction`, unsupported/nil service paths).

`c2c_service.go` includes contract-critical parsing and dispatch (`ProcessC2CMessage`, `ParseC2CAction`, `Execute`), but this batch scope contains no corresponding tests for those execution-time paths.
The README explicitly requires deterministic unit coverage for upload/wifi websocket contract paths as part of quality-gate intent.

Several listed files combine orchestration, persistence, transport, and domain logic in the same module (for example, `goseg/handler/system/support.go` and `goseg/config/config.go` contain long handler/setup paths with broad side effects).

The `goseg/config` package is both the bootstrap/runtime ownership boundary and multiple feature-specific writers (MC/version files), which creates structural coupling where independent concerns share mutable global state.

The same path-selection pattern for mmc/media placement is duplicated across multiple packages (`config`, `logger`, `handler/system`, `system/disk`), making behavior drift and local fixes inconsistent.
Container readiness sequencing is implemented inline in multiple orchestrators with ad-hoc polling loops and repeated status checks, increasing drift across lifecycle paths.
The batch shows repeated patterns of `_`-discarded errors at I/O and lookup boundaries, which converts real failures into silent fallback behavior.
Observed in cross-module paths: config loading, broadcast enrichment, container discovery, and runtime initialization all drop underlying error values and continue with potentially unsafe defaults.
This undermines consistent failure semantics and makes it hard to distinguish recoverable absence from infrastructure/read/write faults.

findings.summary: Command action dispatch patterns are reimplemented per module, increasing the chance of cross-module contract drift as websocket actions evolve.
evidence: `uploadsvc/service.go` uses a private `actionBindings` slice with `parseAction`, `SupportedActions`, and `DescribeAction` to validate and execute actions.
`system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` mirrors that shape with `c2cActionBindings`, `ParseC2CAction`, and `SupportedC2CActions`.
The adapter test asserts mapping details directly against `uploadsvc.OpenEndpointRequest`/`ActionOpenEndpoint`, tightly coupling test-level expectations to a specific internal action shape.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared command-contract abstraction for websocket action registries (including canonical action metadata, parsing, supported-action enumeration, and operation description), then implement module-specific adapters against that boundary to prevent duplicated contract logic and detect drift when action sets change.

findings.summary: Broadcast uses mutable package-level function/variable seams that leak test-only indirection into production behavior.
evidence: `broadcast.go` exposes mutable globals used as seams (for example loop-related helpers and configurable constructors) that are reassigned in tests, making runtime behavior depend on shared mutable package state.
`leak/broadcast.go` also maintains package-global channels/state (`BytesChan`, status sink structures) and ad-hoc sender wiring instead of explicit instance-scoped collaborators.
The seam pattern is uneven across files in the same area, so some paths go through helpers while others call concrete implementations directly.
findings.suggestion: Replace process-global seam vars with an explicit `BroadcastRuntime` interface and inject an instance into loop/sender startup. Keep only immutable dependencies in `loop` and `send` structs, and pass sink/state per call path so tests can substitute mocks without mutating package-level globals.

findings.summary: Runtime seam abstractions are duplicated across packages with low leverage and identical intent.
evidence: `routines/runtime_seams.go` defines a large function-field-backed runtime adapter pattern (`dockerRoutineRuntime`-style struct) that mirrors seams in `docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`.
Both files provide test-oriented overrides instead of small, behavior-bearing interfaces, increasing indirection layers without introducing reusable policy or translation logic.
Callers still reach through package-specific wrappers for similar concerns, so the abstraction duplication does not reduce coupling across subsystem boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate shared seam concerns into a single typed seam package or minimal interfaces colocated at subsystem boundary; remove duplicated adapter stacks and inject dependencies explicitly at startup. Prefer behavior-rich interfaces (one method per responsibility) over function-bag structs.

findings.summary: Config is passed as broad bags, forcing callers into brittle field assertions and weakening API boundaries.
evidence: Config domain types in `structs/configs.go` expose loosely typed fields/structures that downstream modules then downcast and inspect directly.
Callers in importer/shipworkflow/lifecycle pass broad config/context objects through multiple layers rather than explicit capability-specific options.
This pattern appears repeatedly (`container`, `wireguard`, and workflow paths) and ties unrelated package internals to implementation-level keys.
findings.suggestion: Introduce domain-specific option interfaces/types for each operation (e.g., `VersionOptions`, `LifecycleConfig`, `WireguardOptions`) and pass only required fields. This removes repeated assertions and clarifies each abstraction boundary.

findings.summary: Upload and C2C modules duplicate command-contract plumbing, increasing cross-module contract drift risk.
evidence: `uploadsvc/service.go` defines `Action`, `parseAction`, `actionBindings`, and `SupportedActions` to model protocol actions.
`system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` defines `C2CAction`, `ParseC2CAction`, `c2cActionBindings`, and `SupportedC2CActions` with the same contract pattern.
Both are governed by the same compatibility/runtimes contract intent in `README.md` but have no shared contract abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared command-contract helper in a dedicated package (for example `goseg/contracts/ws`), with typed action metadata and common parser/dispatcher adapters, then convert both modules to consume the shared registration and describe operations from a single source of truth to prevent independent drift.

findings.summary: Contract execution paths for upload and Wi‑Fi actions are under-tested despite runtime gate expectations.
evidence: The only upload test in scope checks payload mapping, but does not exercise `Executor` dispatch, unsupported action behavior, nil service handling, or `DescribeAction` in `uploadsvc/service.go`.
The Wi‑Fi C2C service includes JSON unmarshalling, action parsing, and dispatch in `ProcessC2CMessage`, but no C2C contract tests exist in the scoped files.
README’s governance expectations call out deterministic/unit verification for upload and websocket contract flows.
findings.suggestion: Add focused tests for `uploadsvc.Executor` and `system/wifi/service.C2CService`: test happy path, unsupported actions, malformed payloads, nil/invalid service factories, and operation-descriptor paths to pin contract behavior and satisfy command-compatibility expectations.

findings.summary: Split `config` into boot/runtime state and feature-specific config writers to avoid mixed responsibilities
evidence: `goseg/config/config.go` owns process bootstrap (`init()`, `initializeConfig`, `initializePaths`), mutable global runtime state (`globalConfig`, `BasePath`, `Conf()`, multiple mutexes), persistence (`persistConf`, `UpdateConfTyped`), and domain snapshots for every subsystem.

`goseg/config/mc.go` and `goseg/config/version.go` mutate feature-specific config files by directly reading global config state (`BasePath`, `confForMc`, `getVersionChannelForMc`) and performing file I/O in the same package.
A single change in config loading or storage layout requires touching global and feature behavior in the same module family, making separation and testability weaker.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit boundaries: a `configbootstrap` module for startup and env/path setup, a `configstore` module for typed config persistence/state synchronization, and per-feature adapters (`mc`, `version`) that receive dependencies rather than reaching into globals.

findings.summary: Refactor bug-report HTTP handler into a dedicated coordinator plus smaller domain services
evidence: `SupportHandler` performs request decode, path initialization, artifact collection (`dumpBugReport`), optional CPU profiling, archive creation, HTTP upload (`sendBugReport`), status transitions, and sleep-based transition emissions in one flow.
The same file also contains low-level helpers for zip creation, JSON sanitization, path selection, and file system cleanup, tightly coupling API handling with transport and packaging internals.
This coupling makes handler behavior hard to reason about and difficult to unit-test without broad side effects.
findings.suggestion: Create a `support` domain service with explicit steps: `SupportPayload` validation, `BugReportBundleAssembler`, and `BugReportTransport`, and keep `SupportHandler` as only request binding and status orchestration.

findings.summary: Separate auth token crypto, transport request handling, and session persistence into distinct components
evidence: `goseg/auth/auth.go` mixes token encryption/decryption (`KeyfileEncrypt`, `KeyfileDecrypt`), HTTP/WebSocket identity extraction (`requestIdentityFromRequest`), in-memory websocket map operations (`ClientManager`, `WsIsAuthenticated`, `WsNilSession`), and durable session persistence (`AddSession` calling config updates).
`ValidateAndAuthorizeRequestToken` and `CreateToken` combine HTTP request identity parsing, crypto, session map updates, and storage updates in a single file path.
The persistence touchpoints (`AddSession` -> `config.UpdateConfTyped`) force auth changes to couple directly to config implementation details.
findings.suggestion: Extract auth into: (1) transport/context adapter, (2) token crypto strategy, (3) session-state repository for persistence, and keep websocket map updates in a dedicated connection lifecycle service.

findings.summary: Centralize mountpoint/media-path selection to avoid divergent mmc/data placement behavior
evidence: `goseg/handler/system/support.go:makeBugReportPath` scans `gopsutil.disk.Partitions` and walks parent directories with `path.Dir` to infer mmc/media placement.
`goseg/logger/logger.go:makeLogPath` performs the same outer-loop mountpoint + `strings.Contains(p.Device, "mmc")` decision path with slightly different defaults.
`goseg/config/config.go:GetStoragePath` and `checkIsEMMCMachine` use another independent variant of the same environment and mountpoint checks.
`goseg/system/disk.go:IsMountedMMC` duplicates another traversal variant (`listPartitions` + parent loop) for storage placement.
These copies diverge in fallback values and absolute-path handling, so future policy changes can create silent inconsistencies.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared storage placement service (e.g., `platform/storage_path.go`) with a single tested API for base path, media-path resolution, and fallback strategy, then use it across these modules.

findings.summary: Replace ad-hoc readiness polling loops with a shared container wait abstraction
evidence: Both MinIO and netdata container config builders repeatedly poll `getContainerRunningStatus("wireguard")` in local loops waiting for status text containing `"Up"`.
The loop shape (status fetch, contains-check, sleep/poll) is repeated instead of shared behind one utility with timeout/error semantics.
The orchestration layer now has multiple bespoke timing and blocking strategies, which increases drift across service startup behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add a testable `ContainerReadiness` utility (with timeout and backoff) and route MinIO/netdata/volume-writer startup dependencies through it to standardize behavior and eliminate duplicated loop control code.

findings.summary: WireGuard config load ignores `ReadAll` error, allowing JSON decode on partial or empty bytes without surfacing root cause
evidence: goseg/config/wireguard.go:34 reads file contents with `byteValue, _ := ioutil.ReadAll(configFile)` and does not check the I/O error.

goseg/system/system.go has similar silent failures in the same subsystem flow (e.g., `files, _ := ioutil.ReadDir(labelDir)` and `resolvedPath, _ := os.Readlink(fullPath)`) where partial/empty results are later consumed without fault propagation.
Both sites return normal values on malformed read-path outcomes, so callers cannot distinguish invalid input from normal data.
findings.suggestion: Handle all I/O errors explicitly: return wrapped errors from `GetWgConf()` and disk-label helpers, and fail fast or fallback only with explicit and consistent error semantics.

findings.summary: Broadcast state enrichment discards desk code retrieval failures and treats them as empty values, masking runtime regressions
evidence: goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go:280 assigns `lusCode, _ := click.GetLusCode(pier)` and returns `lusCode` without surfacing lookup errors.

Nearby callers in `system/system.go` also convert lookup failures to silently empty values in label and mount resolution paths, creating inconsistent error behavior across related status-reporting helpers.
This means callers cannot tell whether missing data is due to a genuine empty state or an upstream error.
findings.suggestion: Change `lusCodeIfRunning` to return `(string, error)` and propagate or explicitly annotate non-fatal failures; ensure callers preserve a consistent contract when `click.GetLusCode` fails.

findings.summary: Container discovery failures are treated as non-existent containers, enabling wrong control flow in lifecycle management
evidence: goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:240 uses `existingContainer, _ := FindContainer(plan.Name)` and proceeds as if absent when discovery fails.
goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:386 similarly ignores lookup errors before deleting/recreating MinIO containers.
goseg/docker/orchestration/minio.go:203 ignores `rt.getContainerRunningStatus("mc")` and can loop indefinitely while always treating status as unavailable due transient failures.
findings.suggestion: Handle `FindContainer`/`getContainerRunningStatus` errors explicitly: abort lifecycle/recovery operations on real discovery faults, and in the MinIO wait loop return a bounded timeout or failure instead of infinite polling.

findings.summary: Initialization ignores embed sub-filesystem setup error, leaving HTTP file serving with a potentially zero-value FS
evidence: goseg/main.go:58 declares `webContent, _ = fs.Sub(content, "web")` and ignores the returned error.
Subsequent `http.FileServer(http.FS(webContent))` uses that value immediately, so an embed path mismatch would not fail deterministically at startup.
findings.suggestion: Capture and handle `fs.Sub` error in `init`/startup; log it and fail startup if web assets are not available to avoid serving a broken web handler.

findings.summary: Broadcasts authorized state twice and checks marshal error too late
evidence: `BroadcastToClients` calls `auth.ClientManager.BroadcastAuth(authJson)` before `err` is checked, then checks error and calls `BroadcastAuth` again at `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go:432-437`.
`runBroadcastTick` invokes `BroadcastToClients` every tick when auth/clients are present, so duplicates are repeatedly emitted (`goseg/broadcast/loop.go:9-13`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go:24-29`).
findings.suggestion: Marshal to JSON first, return on error, and call `BroadcastAuth` exactly once with the serialized payload. Add a regression test in `goseg/broadcast` for single-send behavior on each tick.

findings.summary: Broadcast package acts as a cross-domain orchestrator instead of a focused boundary
evidence: `broadcast.go` imports `auth`, `backupsvc`, `click`, `config`, `docker`, `leak`, `startram`, and `system` together (`goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go:6-15`).
The package both owns domain state assembly and publishes transport events (`BroadcastToClients`, leak sink wiring, global maps/channels).
Routers/handlers consume handlers from multiple domains while broadcast remains a central fan-out coordinator (`goseg/handler/router/leak.go:14-22`, `goseg/handler/ship/urbit.go:1-3`).
findings.suggestion: Split broadcast into explicit layers: a collector layer that only builds immutable snapshots and a publishing layer that handles transport; inject collaborators via narrow interfaces so the package no longer depends on unrelated domain modules.

findings.summary: Malformed click CLI responses can panic due to unchecked string slicing
evidence: Both `filterResponse` variants do `line[:endIndex]` before verifying `endIndex` (`goseg/click/filter.go:28-33`, `goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go:79-84`).
The parser is used in high-throughput command execution paths that should fail safely on bad output (`goseg/click/notify/notify.go:65-82`, `goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go:108-120`).
findings.suggestion: Create a shared parser that validates `]` existence before slicing and returns explicit parse errors, then route both parser callsites through it.

findings.summary: Duplicate response parser implementations create fragile, inconsistent click behavior
evidence: Nearly identical parse branches for `success`, `code`, and `desk` live in both parser functions (`goseg/click/filter.go:17-61`, `goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go:66-106`).
Both modules expose separate surfaces while producing same outcomes, increasing maintenance cost and the chance of divergence.
findings.suggestion: Collapse parser behavior into one internal package-level helper used by both files, then add table-driven tests for malformed and edge-case lines.

findings.summary: Filesystem cleanup in Hoon file helpers swallows failure states
evidence: `deleteHoon` ignores `os.Stat` errors other than `os.IsNotExist` and ignores `os.Remove` errors completely (`goseg/click/hoon.go:40-42`).
The same pattern exists in `runtime.DeleteHoon` (`goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go:34-40`), so multiple call chains inherit silent failure behavior.
findings.suggestion: Return errors from `DeleteHoon` and `create/execute` paths, then propagate or log at callers where cleanup failures should affect command/error semantics; at minimum handle and report unexpected `Stat`/`Remove` failures.

findings.summary: Bug report upload drops server error payload when status is non-200
evidence: On non-200 responses, `sendBugReport` formats error with `%s` and `resp.Body` instead of reading body bytes (`goseg/handler/system/support.go:419-420`).
This discards actionable response details and can make upload failures harder to debug.
findings.suggestion: Read and close the response body into a bounded buffer on non-success, wrap with status code, and add request timeout/response-body assertions in tests.
